23.text: Paged attention borrows an operating-systems idea, but it still lives inside ordinary transformer math, so the numerical pieces remain familiar.

24.text: Use the same names corpus so the story stays on memory layout and cache management rather than drifting into a different task.

25.text: This lab mixes model behavior with cache-allocation behavior, so controlling randomness keeps the paging story readable.

27.text: A stable run matters here because we want to watch page allocation and sharing as a deterministic systems story, not as a moving target.

32.text: Locate the shared corpus. The model is familiar; the novelty is how its KV history gets laid out in memory.

33.text: Fetch the corpus if needed so the lab remains runnable end to end, including the cache-allocation demonstrations.

34.text: Only bring in the downloader if the dataset is missing. The real systems lesson starts after the model begins allocating KV storage.

36.text: A small corpus is enough here because the important scaling problem is not training size, but how many requests and prefixes the cache must hold efficiently.

37.text: Fetch the names dataset from the URL and save it to the local data directory.

39.text: Read the file line by line, stripping whitespace. Each line is one name that becomes a training document.

41.text: Show how many names we loaded so we can verify the dataset is intact.

43.text: Collect all unique characters from the dataset and sort them to build a deterministic character-to-ID vocabulary.

46.text: Display the character vocabulary size including the special BOS/EOS token.

51.text: Each token maps to a 16-dimensional vector. Small but sufficient for learning character-level name patterns in this demo.

52.text: Split the embedding dimension across four attention heads for multi-head attention.

53.text: Single layer, keeps training fast for the paging demo.

59.text: Same Value autograd engine from Lab 01, exact copy. Each node is a scalar that tracks its computation graph for automatic differentiation.

60.text: __slots__ for memory efficiency, especially matters here because we'll have thousands of Value nodes during training.

62.text: Constructor: scalar data, parent nodes, and local derivatives.

63.text: The scalar computed during the forward pass. Gradients flow backward from the loss to update this value during training.

65.text: Child nodes in the computation graph.

68.text: Define addition between two Value nodes, tracking both operands for backpropagation.

70.text: New node: sum of data, both local grads are 1.

72.text: Define multiplication between two Values using the product rule: d(a*b)/da = b and d(a*b)/db = a, tracked for backprop.

73.text: Wrap raw Python numbers into Value nodes so arithmetic works uniformly in the graph.

74.text: Product rule: d(a*b)/da = b, d(a*b)/db = a.

76.text: Raise a Value to a constant power, with the derivative following the power rule.

77.text: Natural logarithm with derivative 1/x. Essential for computing cross-entropy loss, which is -log(predicted probability).

78.text: Exponential function, unique in that it's its own derivative. Used inside softmax to convert logits to positive values.

79.text: ReLU: gradient is 1 if positive, 0 if negative.

80.text: Negate a Value by multiplying it by -1, reusing the multiplication operator.

81.text: Support expressions like 3 + value by forwarding to the normal add method.

82.text: Subtract two Values by adding the negation, reusing existing operators.

83.text: Handle number - value by negating the Value and adding, reusing existing operators to keep the autograd graph simple.

84.text: Handle number * value by delegating to the Value's own multiplication, ensuring the computation graph tracks both operands.

85.text: Rewrite division as multiplication by the inverse power, keeping the autograd graph simple by reusing __pow__ and __mul__.

86.text: Support expressions like 3 / value by forwarding to the inverse-power path.

89.text: Sort all nodes so each is processed after everything that depends on it. This ordering makes backpropagation a simple reverse walk.

90.text: Keep a set of already-visited nodes to avoid processing any node twice when the graph has diamond-shaped shared subexpressions.

91.text: Depth-first search that builds the topological ordering by visiting children before parents, matching the reverse flow of gradients.

92.text: If we have already processed this node, skip it to avoid duplicate traversal.

93.text: Add this node to the visited set so we do not process it again.

94.text: Recurse into this node's children first, ensuring they appear earlier in the ordering and receive gradients before this node.

95.text: Recurse into each child node to ensure they appear before this node in the ordering.

96.text: After all children are processed, append this node so it comes last in topological order.

98.text: The gradient of the loss with respect to itself is 1 by definition. This seed value propagates backward through the entire graph.

99.text: Walk the sorted nodes in reverse: from the loss back toward parameters, applying the chain rule at each step.

100.text: Chain rule: multiply local grad by parent grad.

104.text: Weight matrix factory: creates a 2D list of small random Value nodes.

108.text: Set up the nested dictionary that holds all weight matrices: embeddings, per-layer attention projections, and MLP weights.

109.text: Token embeddings: map token IDs to vectors.

110.text: Position embeddings: map positions to vectors.

111.text: Separate output head (no weight tying in this lab).

112.text: Close the initial state dict after adding embedding and output head weights. Per-layer weights are added in the loop below.

113.text: For each transformer layer, allocate the four attention projections (Q, K, V, output) and the two MLP weight matrices.

114.text: Compact loop over the four attention projections.

118.text: Flatten all parameters into a single list for the optimizer.

119.text: Display the total number of trainable scalar parameters so we can verify the model size matches our expectations.

122.text: Matrix-vector multiply: dot product of each weight row with input.

123.text: Returns a vector of dot products.

126.text: Dual-mode softmax: works with both Value nodes (training) and plain floats (inference).

127.text: Type dispatch: check if we're in autograd mode.

128.text: Max subtraction for numerical stability (Value mode).

129.text: Raise e to each shifted logit. The prior max-subtraction keeps the largest exponent at 0, preventing numerical overflow.

132.text: Use Python's built-in math.exp for fast exponentiation in plain float mode.

133.text: Sum all exponentiated values to get the normalizing constant. Dividing each exp by this total yields probabilities summing to 1.

134.text: Divide each exponentiated value by the total, producing a proper probability distribution where all values sum to 1.

138.text: Calculate the inverse root-mean-square in one shot: square all elements, average, add epsilon, raise to the -0.5 power.

139.text: Multiply each element by the computed scale factor, bringing the vector to roughly unit magnitude while preserving its direction.

142.text: Dual-mode ReLU: dispatches to Value.relu() or plain max.

143.text: Detect whether the input is a Value node (training with autograd) or a plain float (inference), to call the right implementation.

144.text: Value mode: use the tracked relu for gradient computation.

145.text: Plain float mode: simple max(0, x).

153.text: Contiguous KV cache: the traditional approach. Pre-allocates max_seq_len slots per layer upfront, regardless of actual sequence length. Simple but wasteful, like malloc'ing the maximum possible size for every request.

156.text: Constructor: specify max length, layers, and dimension.

157.text: Remember the maximum sequence length so we know how many slots to pre-allocate in the contiguous KV buffer.

158.text: Track how many transformer layers need separate K and V caches, since each layer maintains its own attention history.

159.text: The dimensionality of each key and value vector, determining how many floats each cache slot holds.

160.text: Pre-allocate full K cache: n_layers × max_seq_len × dims. All slots allocated even if most stay empty.

161.text: Pre-allocate full V cache: same shape, same waste.

162.text: Track how many positions are actually filled.

163.text: Total allocated slots, this number stays fixed regardless of actual usage, which is the waste PagedAttention solves.

164.text: A string tag to identify this cache type in printed output and comparisons.

166.text: Reset for a new sequence, just moves the length counter back to zero.

167.text: "Free" memory by resetting the pointer. The pre-allocated arrays stay in memory.

169.text: Append a new K,V pair to the cache at the current position.

170.text: Write K vector to the next open slot.

171.text: Write V vector to the same slot.

172.text: Only increment length after the last layer writes, since all layers share the same position counter.

173.text: Move the write pointer forward by one position so the next token's KV pair goes into the next slot.

175.text: Retrieve all cached K,V pairs up to the current position.

176.text: Return slices, only the filled portion, not the empty pre-allocated tail.

178.text: Report what fraction of pre-allocated memory actually holds real data, exposing the waste inherent in fixed-size allocation.

179.text: Count how many KV slots are actually filled with real data from the sequence.

180.text: Ratio of used to allocated. For short sequences in a large buffer, this is often just 10-20%.

183.text: Block size for paged allocation: 4 tokens per block. Like a page size in virtual memory, the fundamental unit of allocation.

186.text: PagedKVCache: the core innovation behind vLLM. Instead of pre-allocating max_seq_len per sequence, allocate small fixed-size blocks on demand. A block table maps logical positions to physical block IDs, exactly like OS page tables map virtual addresses to physical frames.

193.text: Constructor: takes total physical blocks available, layers, dimensions, and block size.

194.text: Record how many tokens fit in each physical block, which determines the granularity of memory allocation and waste.

195.text: Store the number of transformer layers this cache must track KV pairs for.

196.text: The number of floats per key or value vector, used to size each slot within a physical block.

197.text: Total physical blocks in the pool.

198.text: Physical K storage: one block = block_size_tokens × dims. Blocks are the atomic allocation unit.

199.text: Physical V storage: same shape as K blocks.

200.text: Free block list, like the OS free frame list. Blocks are allocated from here on demand and returned when sequences finish.

201.text: Block tables: maps (seq_id, layer) → list of physical block IDs. This IS the page table.

202.text: Dictionary mapping each active sequence ID to how many tokens it has cached so far, used for slot addressing.

204.text: A string tag to identify this cache type in printed output and comparisons.

207.text: Out of blocks = out of memory. In production vLLM, this triggers request preemption.

208.text: Raise an error if the pool is exhausted.

209.text: Pop from the free list and return the physical block ID.

210.text: Allocate one physical block from the free list.

211.text: Initialize a new sequence's page table, one empty block list per layer.

212.text: Create the page table: each layer gets its own list of physical block mappings.

214.text: Every new sequence starts empty with zero cached tokens. Blocks will be allocated on demand as tokens arrive.

215.text: Append K,V to the paged cache, the hot path that makes paging work.

216.text: Current sequence length determines which logical block and slot.

219.text: If this logical block doesn't have a physical block yet, allocate one. This is the demand paging, blocks are allocated only when first touched, not upfront.

220.text: Allocate a physical block from the free pool.

221.text: Map: logical block → physical block in the page table.

222.text: Record the allocation event in the log so we can trace exactly when and why each physical block was claimed.

223.text: Which logical block this position falls in (integer division).

225.text: Look up the physical block ID for this logical block.

226.text: Write K vector into the physical block at the right slot.

227.text: Write V vector into the same slot.

228.text: Increment length only after the last layer.

229.text: Increment the sequence position counter so the next token's KV pair lands in the correct slot.

231.text: Reconstruct the full K,V history by walking the page table, translating logical positions to physical locations.

232.text: Look up how many tokens this sequence has cached so far, which tells us how many KV pairs to gather.

235.text: Logical block index for this position.

236.text: Position modulo block_size gives the slot offset within the block, since each block holds block_size consecutive tokens.

237.text: Translate logical → physical via the page table. This indirection is the price of paging, but it's O(1).

238.text: Read K vector from the physical block.

239.text: Read V vector from the physical block.

240.text: Reassemble the full K and V history by walking the page table to translate each logical position into a physical block and slot.

242.text: Free a sequence's blocks back to the pool, like deallocating pages in an OS.

243.text: Collect all physical block IDs from every layer's page table and return them to the free pool for reuse by other sequences.

244.text: Extend the free list, blocks are immediately available for other sequences.

246.text: Delete the length entry for this sequence since it no longer exists in the cache after deallocation.

248.text: Report what fraction of the physical block pool is currently in use, which tracks actual memory consumption much more tightly than contiguous.

250.text: Ratio: with paging, this tracks actual usage closely. No wasted pre-allocation.

253.text: Prefix sharing: the killer feature of paged caches. Multiple sequences share the same physical blocks for a common prefix, zero-copy, just duplicate the page table entries.

254.text: Set up a fresh page table for the destination sequence so it can start receiving shared block references.

255.text: Ceiling division: how many blocks the shared prefix spans.

256.text: Track how many physical blocks are being shared, so we can report the memory savings from prefix sharing.

257.text: Iterate over each transformer layer to copy its block table entries.

258.text: For each block in the prefix...

259.text: Look up which physical block holds this part of the source sequence's prefix in the page table.

260.text: Point the destination's page table at the SAME physical block. No data copy, both sequences read from identical memory.

262.text: Set the destination sequence's cached length to match the prefix, since it inherits all those positions from the source.

263.text: Report the total number of physical blocks that were shared, letting the caller calculate the memory savings.

264.text: Increment the shared block counter each time we point the destination at an existing physical block.

271.text: Unified GPT forward pass: processes one token at a time using any cache implementation. The cache abstraction means the model code doesn't know or care whether it's paged or contiguous.

272.text: Default to the training state dict if no weights provided.

273.text: If no separate inference weights are passed in, use the training state dict. This lets the same function work for both phases.

274.text: Embed: look up token and position vectors, add them.

278.text: Normalize activations before the attention projections, keeping input scale consistent for stable Q/K/V computation.

279.text: Compute Q, K, V projections in one compact expression using a generator.

280.text: Store K,V in the cache, the cache handles the allocation strategy transparently.

281.text: Retrieve all historical K,V pairs, the cache translates logical to physical positions if paged.

282.text: Empty list that will collect each head's output before concatenation into the full multi-head attention result.

283.text: Process each attention head independently, since heads operate on separate slices of the embedding and don't interact.

284.text: Compute the starting index for this head's slice within the full embedding vector.

285.text: Slice out this head's query from the full projection vector, isolating the dimensions this head is responsible for.

288.text: Scaled dot-product attention against all cached keys.

289.text: Convert the raw dot-product scores into a probability distribution over all cached positions via softmax.

290.text: Blend value vectors weighted by the attention probabilities, producing a context-aware summary for this head.

293.text: Add the attention output back to the pre-attention snapshot, allowing gradients to flow through both the attention path and the skip connection.

294.text: Snapshot before the MLP transforms it. The residual connection will add this back so the MLP can only refine the representation.

295.text: Normalize activations before the feed-forward network so the MLP weights see well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

298.text: Contract the activated hidden state back to the embedding dimension via the second MLP weight matrix.

299.text: Add the MLP output to the pre-MLP snapshot, completing the second residual connection in this transformer layer.

300.text: Map the final hidden state to a score for every token in the vocabulary, producing the logits used for next-token prediction.

301.text: Normalize the combined token-plus-position embedding before feeding it into the transformer layers.

302.text: Snapshot the current representation before attention modifies it. The residual connection adds this back to preserve the original signal.

303.text: Append this head's output to the growing list. Once all heads finish, these slices get concatenated into a full vector.

304.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back to the model's embedding dimension.

305.text: Expand the hidden state to four times the embedding dimension via the first MLP weight matrix.

306.text: Adam optimizer settings: learning rate, momentum decay rates (beta1 for mean, beta2 for variance), and epsilon to prevent division by zero.

307.text: Buffer for the exponential moving average of each parameter's gradient (first moment), initialized to zero for all parameters.

308.text: Buffer for the exponential moving average of each parameter's squared gradient (second moment), initialized to zero for all parameters.

309.text: Train for 1000 gradient steps, which is enough for this small model to learn basic character-level name patterns.

310.text: ReLU activation (dispatches to correct mode via relu()).

311.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step with teacher forcing.

312.text: Pick the next training name by cycling through the shuffled dataset with a modulo index.

314.text: Limit the sequence to the maximum context window the model can handle.

315.text: Create a contiguous cache for this training step. Training always uses contiguous, paging is an inference optimization.

316.text: Reset the contiguous cache to empty for this training step's sequence, clearing the position counter to zero.

317.text: Collect the cross-entropy loss at each token position. We'll average them to get the training signal for this step.

318.text: Feed each token one at a time in sequence order, caching KV pairs, matching how inference will run with the cache.

319.text: Input and target for this position.

320.text: Forward pass through the model with the cache.

321.text: Turn raw logit scores into probabilities via softmax so we can compute the cross-entropy against the correct next token.

322.text: Cross-entropy loss: negative log probability of the correct next token.

324.text: Compute gradients of the loss with respect to every parameter via backpropagation.

325.text: Reduce the learning rate linearly toward zero as training progresses, allowing fine-grained convergence at the end.

327.text: Update the running average of gradients (first moment). This smooths out noisy per-step gradients for more stable updates.

330.text: Correct the second moment estimate for the same zero-initialization bias, ensuring accurate variance estimates early in training.

331.text: Apply the Adam update rule to move this parameter one step toward lower loss.

332.text: Zero out this parameter's gradient so it does not accumulate across training steps.

333.text: Print the training loss at regular intervals so we can monitor convergence.

334.text: Display the current training step and loss value so we can monitor convergence during the training loop.

335.text: Update the running average of squared gradients (second moment). This adapts the learning rate per-parameter based on gradient history.

336.text: Correct the first moment estimate for initialization bias, since starting at zero makes early estimates too small.

339.text: Strip autograd wrappers: extract plain floats for fast inference.

342.text: Generate a name using any cache implementation. The cache parameter abstracts the allocation strategy completely.

343.text: Reset the cache for a fresh sequence, clearing any previously stored KV pairs and resetting internal pointers.

344.text: Start with BOS, empty sample buffer.

346.text: Forward pass using plain-float weights and the provided cache.

348.text: Draw one token from the probability distribution to decide the next character.

349.text: The BOS token doubles as an end-of-sequence signal. When the model predicts it, the name is complete and generation stops.

350.text: End-of-sequence detected, so stop generating further tokens for this name.

351.text: Add the newly generated token to the output list, building the name character by character.

352.text: Return both the generated token list and the cache object so the caller can inspect memory utilization and allocation patterns.

358.text: Section header for the comparison experiment.

360.text: Allocate a contiguous cache sized for the full context window, pre-reserving all slots even though most will stay empty.

361.text: Lock the random seed before generation so both cache strategies see identical sampling decisions for a fair comparison.

364.text: Query the cache's utilization stats to see how many of the pre-allocated slots actually hold real data.

365.text: Print the generated name and token count.

366.text: Show allocated vs. used slots, the waste is visible here.

369.text: Allocate a paged cache backed by the same total memory capacity, but using on-demand block allocation instead of upfront reservation.

370.text: Same seed ensures identical random choices.

371.text: Generate a name using the paged cache. The output should be identical since only the allocation strategy differs, not the computation.

372.text: Convert the generated token IDs back to characters so we can display the name and visually compare it against the contiguous version.

373.text: Display the name generated with the paged cache and its token count for comparison.

374.text: Show blocks allocated on demand, much less waste than contiguous.

375.text: Assert outputs match, the cache strategy must not affect model output, only memory efficiency.

376.text: Verify that paged and contiguous caches produce the exact same output. The allocation strategy must be invisible to the model's computation.

377.text: Generate a name using the contiguous cache to establish the baseline for memory usage and output comparison.

378.text: 32 physical blocks for the paged cache pool.

382.text: Section header for prefix sharing demo.

383.text: Create a paged cache with plenty of blocks for the prefix sharing demo.

384.text: Tokenize a short prefix "an", simulates a shared system prompt.

385.text: Register the prefix sequence in the paged cache's page table, setting up the block mappings that will be shared with other sequences.

387.text: Forward pass stores K,V in the cache at each position.

388.text: Run each prefix token through the model to populate the KV cache.

389.text: Look up the prefix sequence's length in the cache to verify all tokens were stored correctly before sharing.

390.text: Count how many blocks the prefix occupies.

391.text: Print how many tokens and physical blocks the prefix occupies, showing the baseline cost that sharing will amortize.

393.text: Spawn three new sequences that will share the prefix's physical blocks, demonstrating zero-copy KV reuse.

395.text: Share the prefix's physical blocks with the new sequence, zero data copying, just page table duplication.

396.text: Report how many blocks were shared (should match the prefix's block count).

397.text: Show that sharing adds zero new block allocations.

398.text: Compare: without sharing, we'd need N copies of the prefix blocks.

399.text: For each of the three new sequences, share the prefix blocks and then generate a unique continuation.

409.text: Print a section header before the final batch of generated names to visually separate it from the comparison experiments.

410.text: Large paged cache pool for generating many names.

411.text: Generate 20 names to showcase the model's learned character patterns, each using its own paged cache sequence.

413.text: Generate using the paged cache, each sample gets its own sequence ID.

415.text: Display each generated name on its own line so we can visually inspect the character-level patterns the model learned.

416.text: Free the sequence's blocks back to the pool, memory is recycled for the next sample.

417.text: Unique seed per sample for variety.
